## **Use of the Microscope**

## **Objectives**

In this laboratory session you will learn to use an essential tool in the biologist's kit, the compound light microscope. Carefully read and closely follow the following instructions before you begin this activity in order to avoid damaging the microscope in your care.

## **Your objectives for this lab are:**

-   Identify the parts of the microscope and list the function of each.
-   Demonstrate the proper techniques for use, care, and transportation of the microscope.
-   Define and demonstrate a working understanding of the concepts of total magnification, resolution, parfocal, field diameter, depth of field, and working distance.
-   Apply microscopy skills to properly focus, view, and manipulate a variety of histology slides.
-   Explain why the microscope's field of view decreases as you increase the magnification.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **Terms to Learn**

-   Arm\
-   Neck\
-   Body\
-   Base\
-   Ocular lens\
-   Objective lens\
-   Rotating nosepiece (Turret)\
-   Coarse and Fine focus knobs\
-   Mechanical stage\
-   Mechanical stage knobs\
-   Illumination light source\
-   Iris diaphragm\
-   Condenser adjustment knob

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **General Rules**

-   Always carry the microscope using two hands: one holding the arm and the other supporting the base.\
-   Do not touch the objective lenses. Keep the scanning lens in position when storing.\
-   Clean lenses only with lens paper and lens cleaning solution.\
-   Report malfunctions to your instructor; do not attempt repairs.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **Returning the Microscope**

-   Lower the stage\
-   Rotate to scanning (4x) lens\
-   Remove and clean slide\
-   Wrap cord properly\
-   Replace dust cover\
-   Carry with two hands

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **Prelab Activities**

![Figure 2.1](images/_page_34_Picture_1.jpeg)

**Figure 2.1** Components of a Typical Bright Field Microscope

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### **Prelab Activity 2.1**

|                         |                      |
|-------------------------|----------------------|
| 1\. Ocular lens         | 6\. Mechanical stage |
| 2\. Head                | 7\. Stage clips      |
| 3\. Revolving nosepiece | 8\. Diaphragm        |
| 4\. Objective lens      | 9\. Condenser        |
| 5\. Arm                 | 10\. Fine focus knob |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### **Prelab Activity 2.2**

| Microscope Part     | Definitions |
|---------------------|-------------|
| Condenser           | j           |
| Revolving Turret    | g           |
| Mechanical stage    | q           |
| Ocular lens         | e           |
| Power switch        | p           |
| Iris diaphragm      | k           |
| Arm                 | d           |
| Depth of field      | o           |
| Fine focus          | i           |
| X-Y stage controls  | l           |
| Light source        | a           |
| Total magnification | m           |
| Head                | b           |
| Base                | c           |
| Objective lens      | f           |
| Coarse focus        | h           |
| Field of view       | n           |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **Lab Activities**

## **Magnification**

Student microscopes typically have four objective lenses:

-   Scanning (4x)\
-   Low power (10x)\
-   High power (40x)\
-   Oil immersion (100x)

The ocular lens is typically 10x.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **Lab Activity 2.1 (Magnification Table)**

| Lenses        | Ocular Lens | Objective Lens | Total Magnification |
|---------------|-------------|----------------|---------------------|
| Scanning      | 10x         | 4x             | 40x                 |
| Low Power     | 10x         | 10x            | 100x                |
| High Power    | 10x         | 40x            | 400x                |
| Oil Immersion | 10x         | 100x           | 1000x               |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **Focusing on Specimens**

Follow the three-step focusing process:

### **1. Scanning Lens (4x)**

-   Start with stage lowered\
-   Turn on light\
-   Place specimen\
-   Use coarse focus, then fine focus\
-   Center specimen

### **2. Low Power (10x)**

-   Rotate turret\
-   Use **fine focus only**\
-   Re-center specimen

### **3. High Power (40x)**

-   Rotate to 40x\
-   Use **fine focus only**\
-   Adjust diaphragm and light as needed

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **Field of View Calculations**

### **Measured Values (example)**

1.  Scanning (4x)
    -   4.5 mm\
    -   4500 µm
2.  Low Power (10x)
    -   1.8 mm\
    -   1800 µm

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### **Formula**

Field Diameter × Magnification = Constant

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### **Calculated Values**

3.  High Power (40x)
    -   0.45 mm\
    -   450 µm
4.  Oil Immersion (100x)
    -   0.18 mm\
    -   180 µm

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **Focusing Exercises**

### **Letter “e” Observation**

-   The image appears **upside down and reversed** compared to the original.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### **Depth of Field (Threads)**

-   More adjustment is needed at higher magnification.\
-   At high power, **not all layers are in focus simultaneously**.\
-   This shows that **depth of field decreases as magnification increases**.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **Examining Tissues**

Observe prepared slides at:

-   40x\
-   100x\
-   400x

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **End of Lab**

-   Clean and return slides\
-   Turn off microscope\
-   Wrap cord\
-   Set scanning lens\
-   Lower stage\
-   Cover microscope\
-   Return to cabinet

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **Troubleshooting**

**Image too dark?** - Check power, light, rheostat, diaphragm

**Cannot see clearly?** - Clean lenses\
- Check slide orientation\
- Adjust focus

**Half-moon image?** - Objective not clicked in

**Headache?** - Adjust ocular distance and focus\
- Take breaks

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **Microscopy Glossary**

| Term                   | Definition                               |
|------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| binocular              | microscope with two eyepieces            |
| brightfield microscope | produces dark image on bright background |
| coarse focus           | large adjustments                        |
| fine focus             | small adjustments                        |
| condenser              | focuses light on specimen                |
| magnification          | enlarges image                           |
| resolution             | distinguishes two points                 |
| ocular lens            | eyepiece                                 |
| objective lens         | lens near specimen                       |
| total magnification    | ocular × objective                       |
| field of view          | visible area                             |
| stage                  | platform for slide                       |
| illuminator            | light source                             |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

© 2018 Rice University (OpenStax, CC BY 4.0)
